sanskrit: अनिष्टमिष्टं मिश्रं च त्रिविधं कर्मणः फलम् । भवत्यत्यागिनां प्रेत्य न तु संन्यासिनां क्वचित् ॥
hindiTranslation: कर्मफलका त्याग न करनेवाले मनुष्योंको कर्मोंका इष्ट, अनिष्ट और मिश्रित  ऐसे तीन प्रकारका फल मरनेके बाद भी होता है परन्तु कर्मफलका त्याग करनेवालोंको कहीं भी नहीं होता ॥
englishTranslation: For those who do not renounce the fruits of action, action produces three kinds of results—pleasant, unpleasant, and mixed—even after death; but for those who renounce the fruits, there is no such result anywhere.
essence: The chain of action ends when desire for its fruit ends.
speaker: Krishna
simpleMeaning: If you keep wanting the result of your actions, those actions keep producing effects for you. If you let go of results, that chain ends.
simpleInsight: There is relief in not carrying your actions as personal baggage.
detailedMeaning: On Kurukshetra, Arjuna is frozen between duty and grief. Krishna deepens the lesson on renunciation: action itself is unavoidable, but bondage comes from clinging to its fruit. That is why this verse distinguishes the renouncer from the one still tied to results.
modernRelevance: You send the message, then keep reopening the chat for a reply that decides your mood. The verse points to the hidden chain: craving the result keeps the action alive inside you.
translationLiteral: अनिष्टम् (unpleasant) / इष्टम् (pleasant) / मिश्रम् (mixed) / च (and) / त्रिविधम् (threefold) / कर्मणः (of action) / फलम् (fruit, result) / भवति (becomes, occurs) / अत्यागिनाम् (for those who do not renounce) / प्रेत्य (after death) / न तु (but not) / संन्यासिनाम् (for renouncers) / क्वचित् (anywhere, at any time)
insight: It shifts attention from owning outcomes to releasing them after action.

quoteVariants.text: Cling to results, and action follows you beyond death.
The result follows only the one who keeps it.
